author: @gkdresden
text: It is better to use shape memory alloys insted of rubber. Nitinol is very interesting with its pseudoelastic tension of 8%. Together with its Young modulus of 75–83 GPa of the martensite phase it gives a lot of tension. Using a thin Nitinol wire and a tube around it forming a series configuration of cooler, regenerator and heater gives allready a small but quite powerful heat engine.

author: @johnallenrichter
text: Rob, have you considered replacing the axle's point and strike plate with two opposing magnets?  That setup might keep the axle from going forward and keep the axle in place.  Plus it would be completely frictionless.  You might need  inverted cone magnets matched with a smaller coned magnets on both ends of the axle to make it much more sturdy.   Replace the interior round magnets with  electromagnets that can be controlled variably and strong enough to hold the weight of an additional weighted wheel attached to the axle for angular kinetic energy to keep the axle spinning as long as possible before needing to apply small bursts of energy to bring the rotation speed up.  Without any friction at all, and with fingers crossed, it might possibly eventually lead to zero point energy?  On a small scale?

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
It is through simple demonstrations like that where proof of concept inspires improvements and continues processes that build the foundation of progress.

Fantastic job again Rob!

I still say that a two part cradle for that magnetic bearing would allow it to 'hover' straight up in air for a wind collector 🤷. If I had the stepped motors I would just build it myself easy, but I'm working on a pancake winding configuration in series to see how that reduces back emf on a coil under work conditions.

I'll let you know what I find in the event you want to include it on the channel. I think the results will be promising.

author: @technosaurus3805
text: Those magnetic bearings would have been great on my solar powered motor. Pretty simple to make - just put long, black plastic rods (high thermal expansion) on a bike wheel with weights at the end to balance them and add torque. Then place it where 1 side is in the sun and the other in the shade. Add metal fins and play with the rod shape for some fun science. Just use a chain to hook the sprockets to whatever you want.
